key_insight: Standard MSE training can make an interatomic potential look accurate on held-out energies/forces while still producing physically wrong molecular dynamics samples, so the model must be trained against the distribution it generates.
engineering_relevance: For computational chemistry and materials simulation, this means reliable force fields need distribution-aware calibration, not just pointwise regression accuracy, to produce trustworthy thermodynamics and sampling.
method: Start with a machine-learning potential trained by standard MSE on DFT energies and forces.
Add a post-training contrastive regularization term (CRMSE) based on KL divergence between the model’s Boltzmann distribution and the target.
Use persistent Langevin chains to discover the configurations the model visits spuriously and raise their energy, without adding new ab initio data.
evidence_signal: On ethanol and aspirin from MD17, CRMSE restored energy, distance, and dihedral free-energy distributions to near-quantitative agreement with DFT while keeping energy errors within chemical accuracy and preserving force accuracy.
watch_item: The correction depends on generating meaningful persistent-chain samples, so stability and cost of the sampling step may matter for larger systems or more complex energy landscapes.
paper_title: Contrastive Regularization of Machine Learning Potentials
